candidates.reasoning: The vast, flat steppe landscape with arid, sandy soil and sparse, newly planted vegetation is highly characteristic of the Central Asian plains. The scale of the open horizon and the specific soil composition are common in the Kazakh steppe.

The southern regions of Russia, particularly the steppe zones bordering Kazakhstan, share identical geography, soil color, and flat terrain. It is geographically indistinguishable from Kazakhstan in this specific environment.
The arid, flat plains and sandy soil are consistent with the Mongolian steppe, although the infrastructure style is slightly more common in the former Soviet republics.

The landscape shows a semi-arid steppe environment with sparse, low-lying grasses and newly planted saplings typical of windbreak initiatives in Central Asian plains. This vegetation pattern is common across the Kazakh steppe.

The southern regions of Russia, particularly the steppe zones, exhibit identical botanical characteristics to Kazakhstan, featuring similar drought-resistant grasses and managed afforestation efforts along roadsides.
The arid, flat grassland and sandy soil composition are consistent with the Mongolian steppe, although the presence of organized road-side planting is more common in the aforementioned countries.

The road is a concrete slab construction common in Central Asian steppe regions. The vehicle types and the general layout of the industrial/truck stop area are consistent with Kazakh infrastructure standards.
The concrete road surface and the style of the utility poles on the right are very common across rural Russia. The driving side is right-hand, which matches Russian regulations.
The arid landscape and basic concrete road infrastructure are seen in Mongolia, though the specific truck models are more characteristic of the CIS region.

The combination of the specific concrete utility pole design (slender, grey, slightly tapered) and the style of the flatbed trucks is very common in Central Asia, particularly Kazakhstan. The wide, open steppe landscape combined with this specific infrastructure is highly characteristic of the region.

The concrete utility poles and the types of heavy-duty trucks seen in the background are also prevalent in rural Russia. The infrastructure is nearly identical to that of Kazakhstan, making it a strong candidate for the same region.
Similar utility pole styles and open terrain are found here, though the specific road construction and vehicle types are slightly more aligned with the post-Soviet states.
